name: Euler number
description: Measures pressure drop relative to dynamic pressure. It indicates how strongly pressure forces or losses compare with inertial motion in a flow.

descriptions: Inertial forces dominate. Pressure drop is small compared to dynamic pressure.
Typical for many engineering applications. Both inertial and frictional effects are important.
Idealized limit where inertial and frictional effects are balanced. Useful for theoretical analysis.
Frictional forces dominate. Pressure drop is large compared to dynamic pressure.

name: Froude number
description: Measures flow speed relative to gravity wave speed. It indicates whether gravity can communicate disturbances upstream in free-surface and stratified flows.

descriptions: Flow velocity is less than wave speed. Disturbances can propagate upstream. Common in rivers and open channels.
Flow velocity exceeds wave speed. Disturbances cannot propagate upstream. Occurs in steep channels and spillways.

name: Nusselt number
description: Measures convective heat transfer relative to pure conduction across the same length scale. It indicates how much motion enhances heat transfer above the conductive reference.

descriptions: Heat transfer is close to the purely conductive reference limit. Occurs in stagnant fluids or weakly convective boundary layers.
Convection enhances heat transfer above the purely conductive reference rate. Occurs in moving or well mixed fluids and thin thermal boundary layers.

name: Reynolds number
description: Measures inertial transport relative to viscous resistance. It is the primary indicator of laminar, transitional, and turbulent flow behavior.

descriptions: Smooth, ordered flow. Viscous forces dominate. Velocity profile is parabolic in pipes.
Chaotic, diffusive flow. Inertial forces dominate. Enhanced mixing and heat transfer.

name: Strouhal number
description: Measures oscillation time scale relative to advective flow time scale. It connects periodic motion such as vortex shedding to the speed and size of the flow.

descriptions: Oscillations are rapidly swept by the fast flow.
Periodic vortex shedding patterns.
Localized oscillations dominate the flow. The fluid vibrates or pulsates in place.

name: Weber number
description: Measures inertial stress relative to surface-tension stress. It indicates whether interfaces, jets, and droplets remain cohesive or break up.

descriptions: Surface tension drives axisymmetric capillary instability. Jet breaks into droplets larger than the jet diameter at a distance far downstream.
Aerodynamic forces enhance Rayleigh instability. Droplets form near the jet diameter; breakup length begins to decrease.
Short wavelength surface waves stripped by aerodynamic pressure produce droplets smaller than the jet diameter.
Jet disintegrates immediately at the nozzle exit into a dense spray of very fine droplets. Dominant in fuel injectors.
Droplet oscillates but remains intact. Breakup is marginal and occurs only after many oscillation cycles.
Droplet deforms into a thin bag inflated by the flow, which ruptures into fine droplets with a coarser rim fragment.
Combined bag and stamen breakup. A liquid stamen forms inside the bag, producing a bimodal fragment size distribution.
Liquid is continuously stripped from the droplet equator as thin sheets, forming many small satellite droplets.
Droplet shatters almost instantaneously into a cloud of fine fragments. Relevant to high speed impacts and sprays.

name: Prandtl number
description: Measures momentum diffusivity relative to thermal diffusivity. It indicates the relative thickness of velocity and thermal boundary layers.

descriptions: Thermal diffusivity dominates over momentum diffusion. Thermal boundary layers are much thicker than velocity boundary layers, typical of liquid metals.
Momentum and thermal diffusivities are comparable. Velocity and thermal boundary layers have similar thicknesses, typical of many gases.
Momentum diffusion dominates over thermal diffusion. Thermal boundary layers are thinner than velocity boundary layers, typical of oils and viscous liquids.

name: Mach number
description: Measures flow speed relative to the speed of sound. It indicates whether compressibility, acoustic waves, and shocks are important.

descriptions: Density variations are negligible. Flow can be treated as incompressible.
Compressibility effects are small but may be noticeable in some cases.
Flow speed approaches the speed of sound. Shock waves and strong compressibility effects occur.
Flow speed exceeds the speed of sound. Strong shock waves and compressibility effects dominate.
Flow speed is much greater than the speed of sound. Extreme shock waves, high temperatures, and ionization may occur.

name: Schmidt number
description: Measures momentum diffusivity relative to mass diffusivity. It indicates the relative thickness of velocity and concentration boundary layers.

descriptions: Mass diffusivity dominates over momentum diffusivity. Occurs in liquid metals and dilute gases.
Momentum and mass diffusivities are comparable. Common in many gases.
Typical for liquids, e.g. 600 to 700 for water.

name: Sherwood number
description: Measures convective mass transfer relative to molecular diffusion. It indicates how much flow enhances species transport above the diffusive reference.

descriptions: Mass transfer is dominated by molecular diffusion. Sh ≈ 1 corresponds to the purely diffusive limit.
Convection significantly enhances mass transfer above the purely diffusive rate.

name: Péclet number
description: Measures advective heat transport relative to thermal diffusion. It indicates whether temperature is carried mainly by flow or smoothed by diffusion.

descriptions: Thermal diffusion dominates. Temperature gradients are smoothed quickly relative to the flow timescale.
Advection dominates heat transport. Thermal boundary layers are thin and temperature variations are convected downstream.

name: Fourier number
description: Measures elapsed time relative to the thermal diffusion time across a length scale. It indicates how far a transient temperature disturbance has penetrated.

descriptions: Significant temperature gradients persist throughout the domain. The thermal front has not yet penetrated the full depth.
Temperature gradients are diminishing. The body approaches thermal equilibrium with its surroundings.

name: Capillary number
description: Measures viscous stress relative to surface-tension stress. It indicates whether flow can deform interfaces against capillary restoring forces.

descriptions: Surface tension forces dominate. Droplets and bubbles maintain compact, nearly spherical shapes.
Both surface tension and viscous forces are significant. Interfaces are noticeably deformed.
Viscous forces dominate. Interfaces deform freely and droplets may be highly elongated or broken up.

name: Biot number
description: Measures internal conduction resistance relative to surface convection resistance. It indicates whether a solid can be treated as nearly uniform in temperature.

descriptions: Internal conduction resistance is small compared with surface convection resistance. Temperature gradients inside the solid are negligible and the lumped capacitance model applies.
Internal conduction resistance is significant. Temperature gradients develop inside the solid and the heat equation must usually be solved.
Internal conduction resistance dominates over surface convection resistance. The surface equilibrates rapidly relative to the interior, and strong internal gradients can persist.

name: Bond number
description: Measures gravitational forcing relative to surface tension. It indicates whether interfaces are shaped more by weight or capillarity.

descriptions: Surface tension controls interface shape. Droplets and menisci remain strongly curved by capillary forces.
Gravity controls interface deformation. Large droplets, bubbles, and free surfaces flatten or sag under their own weight.

name: Ohnesorge number
description: Measures viscous damping relative to inertial and capillary effects. It indicates how strongly viscosity suppresses droplet oscillation, pinch off, and breakup.

descriptions: Viscous dissipation is weak. Oscillation, pinch off, and breakup are governed mainly by inertia and surface tension.
Viscous, inertial, and capillary effects are all important. Droplet relaxation and breakup are noticeably damped.
Viscous dissipation dominates. Interfaces relax slowly and capillary waves are strongly suppressed.

name: Laplace number
description: Measures inertial and capillary effects relative to viscous damping. It is the inverse-square counterpart of the Ohnesorge number for free-surface motion.

descriptions: Viscous stresses damp interfacial motion rapidly. Surface waves and breakup are sluggish.
Viscous damping competes with inertia and surface tension. Interface motion depends strongly on geometry and forcing.
Inertia and surface tension dominate over viscous damping. Capillary waves and rapid pinch off are prominent.

name: Galileo number
description: Measures gravity-driven motion relative to viscous diffusion. It is useful for settling, rising bubbles, and natural motion set by body forces.

descriptions: Viscous resistance controls motion. Falling particles or bubbles move slowly and remain in a creeping flow regime.
Gravity driven motion is affected by both viscosity and inertia. Wakes and path instabilities may begin to appear.
Inertial effects dominate gravitational motion. Wakes, vortex shedding, and unsteady trajectories become important.

name: Cauchy number
description: Measures inertial stress relative to elastic or compressibility stress. It indicates when deformation, pressure waves, or material elasticity affect dynamic similarity.

descriptions: Reference stresses are large compared with inertial stresses. Deformation or pressure response controls the motion.
Inertial stresses exceed the reference stress scale. Dynamic deformation and wave effects become significant.

name: Womersley number
description: Measures oscillatory inertia relative to viscous diffusion in unsteady internal flow. It indicates whether velocity profiles can adjust within each cycle.

descriptions: Viscous diffusion spans the flow during each cycle. The velocity profile remains close to the steady laminar shape.
Unsteady inertia and viscosity are both important. Phase lag and profile distortion develop across the section.
Oscillation is too rapid for viscous diffusion to penetrate the full radius. The core moves nearly as a plug with thin oscillatory boundary layers.

name: Courant number
description: Measures the distance information travels in one time step relative to a cell length. It is a stability and accuracy indicator for time-marching numerical schemes.

descriptions: Information travels less than one characteristic length per time step. Explicit advection schemes are more likely to remain stable.
Information travels more than one characteristic length per time step. Many explicit schemes become unstable or inaccurate unless additional treatment is used.

name: Lewis number
description: Measures thermal diffusivity relative to mass diffusivity. It indicates whether heat or species concentration spreads faster.

descriptions: Species concentration disturbances spread faster than temperature disturbances. Mass transfer boundary layers tend to be thicker than thermal boundary layers.
Temperature disturbances spread faster than species concentration disturbances. Thermal boundary layers tend to be thicker than concentration boundary layers.

name: Bejan number
description: Measures pressure driving relative to viscous and thermal diffusion. It characterizes forced-convection strength in pressure-driven heat-transfer problems.

descriptions: Pressure work is small relative to viscous and diffusive transport. Flow forcing has a weak effect on thermal transport.
Pressure work strongly drives transport. Forced convection and pressure losses are central to the thermal behavior.

name: Mass Fourier number
description: Measures elapsed time relative to the mass diffusion time across a length scale. It indicates how far a transient concentration disturbance has penetrated.

descriptions: Concentration gradients remain localized. The diffusing species has not penetrated the full characteristic length.
Diffusion has spread across much of the characteristic length. Concentration gradients are relaxing toward equilibrium.

name: Mass Péclet number
description: Measures advective species transport relative to molecular diffusion. It indicates whether concentration fields are carried mainly by flow or smoothed by diffusion.

descriptions: Molecular diffusion transports species faster than the flow carries them across the characteristic length.
Bulk motion carries species faster than molecular diffusion can smooth concentration gradients.

name: Grashof number
description: Measures buoyancy forcing relative to viscous damping. It is the natural-convection analogue of a Reynolds-number scale based on temperature-induced density differences.

descriptions: Viscous damping keeps buoyancy-driven motion smooth and ordered.
Buoyancy and inertia are strong enough for unsteady plumes and mixed laminar-turbulent behavior to appear.
Buoyancy-driven inertia dominates viscous damping. Natural-convection boundary layers and plumes become turbulent.

name: Rayleigh number
description: Measures buoyancy forcing relative to combined momentum and thermal diffusion. It indicates the onset and strength of natural convection.

descriptions: Thermal diffusion suppresses buoyant motion. Heat transfer is mainly conductive in the classic horizontal-layer reference case.
Buoyant motion is sustained and enhances heat transport above conduction.
Buoyancy-driven flow becomes strongly unsteady and turbulent, producing vigorous mixing and enhanced heat transfer.

name: Richardson number
description: Measures buoyancy relative to inertial forcing. It indicates whether a flow behaves more like forced, mixed, or natural convection.

descriptions: Inertial forcing dominates buoyancy. Temperature differences have a weak effect on the main flow.
Buoyancy and imposed motion both influence the flow and heat-transfer pattern.
Buoyancy dominates over imposed inertia. Flow structure is set mainly by density differences.

name: Stanton number
description: Measures surface heat-transfer rate relative to the thermal capacity advected by a flow. It indicates what fraction of the moving fluid thermal capacity is exchanged.

descriptions: Only a small fraction of the bulk thermal capacity is exchanged with the surface over the reference length.
Surface heat transfer has a measurable effect on the moving fluid temperature.
Surface exchange is strong relative to the advected thermal capacity of the flow.

name: Eckert number
description: Measures kinetic energy relative to sensible enthalpy based on a temperature difference. It indicates when viscous or compressible heating affects the thermal field.

descriptions: Kinetic energy is small compared with the imposed thermal energy scale.
Conversion of kinetic energy into heat can affect temperature predictions, especially near walls or shocks.
Kinetic energy is comparable to or larger than the sensible heat scale. Compressible heating effects are central.

name: Brinkman number
description: Measures viscous heat generation relative to conductive heat removal. It indicates whether dissipation heating must be included in an energy balance.

descriptions: Viscous dissipation produces little heat compared with conductive transport.
Viscous heating can noticeably alter local temperatures and heat-transfer rates.
Viscous dissipation is comparable to or larger than conductive heat removal.

name: Drag coefficient
description: Measures drag force normalized by dynamic pressure and reference area. It compares the aerodynamic or hydrodynamic resistance of bodies across speeds and scales.

descriptions: Drag is small relative to the dynamic pressure force on the reference area, typical of streamlined bodies at favorable conditions.
Drag is a significant fraction of the dynamic pressure force and depends strongly on shape and Reynolds number.
Pressure drag, separation, or form effects are large relative to the reference dynamic pressure force.

name: Lift coefficient
description: Measures lift force normalized by dynamic pressure and reference area. It compares lifting performance across geometries, speeds, and fluid densities.

descriptions: Lift is small relative to the dynamic pressure force on the reference area.
Lift is appreciable and sensitive to angle of attack, shape, and Reynolds number.
Lift is large relative to the reference dynamic pressure force; stall, circulation, or strong pressure differences may dominate behavior.

name: Pressure coefficient
description: Measures static pressure variation normalized by dynamic pressure. It maps local pressure loading on bodies and surfaces in a flow.

descriptions: Static pressure variations are small compared with the dynamic pressure scale.
Pressure loading is an important part of the force balance on the body or surface.
Pressure differences are comparable to or larger than the dynamic pressure scale.

name: Skin-friction coefficient
description: Measures wall shear stress normalized by dynamic pressure. It indicates the frictional part of momentum loss along a surface.

descriptions: Wall shear is small compared with the dynamic pressure scale, as in high Reynolds number streamlined flows.
Wall shear is a measurable part of the momentum balance.
Wall shear is large relative to dynamic pressure, often indicating strong viscous effects or roughness.

name: Cavitation number
description: Measures pressure margin above vapor pressure relative to dynamic pressure. It indicates how close a liquid flow is to forming vapor cavities.

descriptions: Dynamic pressure is large compared with the pressure margin to vapor pressure. Cavitation is likely in low-pressure regions.
Cavitation may appear locally depending on geometry, nuclei, and unsteady pressure fluctuations.
The pressure margin is large relative to dynamic pressure. Cavitation is generally suppressed.

name: Rossby number
description: Measures inertial motion relative to rotational or Coriolis effects. It indicates whether rotation controls the flow dynamics.

descriptions: Coriolis effects dominate inertia. Flow tends toward geostrophic or columnar balance.
Rotation and inertia both influence trajectories, waves, and instabilities.
Inertial motion dominates over rotation. Coriolis deflection is a secondary correction.

name: Ekman number
description: Measures viscous diffusion relative to rotational effects. It indicates the importance of viscous boundary layers in rotating flows.

descriptions: Rotation is strong compared with viscous diffusion. Thin Ekman layers control boundary adjustment.
Viscous diffusion and rotation both influence the flow over the characteristic length.
Viscous diffusion acts faster than rotational adjustment. Rotation has a weak dynamical effect.

name: Taylor number
description: Measures rotational forcing relative to viscous diffusion. It indicates when rotating shear flows become susceptible to Taylor-type instabilities.

descriptions: Viscous diffusion damps rotational shear before strong inertial structures develop.
Rotation competes with viscous diffusion and can alter stability and momentum transport.
Rotational forcing is strong enough that Taylor-like vortices or related instabilities may occur, depending on geometry.

name: Helmholtz number
description: Measures acoustic length scale relative to wavelength through frequency and sound speed. It indicates whether a body or domain is acoustically compact.

descriptions: The object or domain is small relative to the acoustic wavelength and can often be treated as acoustically compact.
Wavelength effects are noticeable and spatial phase variation matters.
The acoustic wavelength is comparable to or smaller than the characteristic length. Resonance and wave structure dominate.

name: Roshko number
description: Measures vortex-shedding frequency relative to viscous diffusion. It combines Strouhal and Reynolds effects for wake oscillations.

descriptions: Viscous diffusion is fast relative to the shedding time scale, suppressing organized vortex formation.
Unsteady shedding and viscous diffusion are comparable, supporting coherent periodic wakes.
Shedding is rapid compared with viscous diffusion and wake dynamics are dominated by inertia.

name: Knudsen number
description: Measures molecular mean free path relative to a macroscopic length. It indicates whether continuum fluid assumptions are valid.

descriptions: Molecular mean free path is much smaller than the geometry. Navier-Stokes continuum models are usually appropriate.
Continuum behavior mostly holds, but velocity slip and temperature jump at boundaries can be important.
Continuum assumptions break down and kinetic effects must be modeled explicitly.
Molecular collisions with boundaries dominate over intermolecular collisions. Free molecular or kinetic descriptions are required.

name: Archimedes number
description: Measures buoyant forcing on a particle or bubble relative to viscous resistance. It indicates the settling or rising regime set by density contrast.

descriptions: Viscous resistance dominates buoyant motion. Particles or bubbles settle slowly in creeping flow.
Buoyancy, viscosity, and inertia are all important. Wake effects and shape sensitivity may appear.
Buoyant forcing is large compared with viscous resistance. Inertial wakes and unsteady paths become important.

name: Shields parameter
description: Measures bed shear stress relative to submerged particle weight. It indicates whether grains on a bed remain stable or begin to move.

descriptions: Bed shear is below the typical threshold for sustained grain motion.
Grains are near the onset of motion; transport depends sensitively on particle Reynolds number, packing, and turbulence.
Bed shear is high enough for sustained sediment motion and bed-load transport.
